paper_title: Cognitive Episodes in LLM Reasoning Traces Enable Interpretable Human Item Difficulty Prediction

claims.claim: Structured episode-level representations from LRM reasoning traces improve human item difficulty prediction over all existing approaches.

evidence.description: Epi2Diff achieves best performance across all four test sets (SAT Math, SAT Reading, Cambridge, USMLE) and all eight reported metrics. SAT Math: ACC 0.730 vs. best baseline 0.698; SAT Reading: ACC 0.631 vs. 0.623; Cambridge: RMSE 7.612 vs. 8.200; USMLE: R² 0.121 vs. 0.076.
8.1% average relative gain over LLM supervised fine-tuning baselines on SAT-derived classification benchmarks.
Epi2Diff outperforms 5 paradigm baselines: SLM fine-tuning, LLM zero-shot, LLM ICL, LLM SFT full-parameter, and LLM SFT LoRA.

claims.claim: Harder items induce more implementation-centered effort, iterative analysis-execution cycles, and larger reasoning scale—not merely longer responses.

evidence.description: Standardized Average Marginal Effects show total trace length (+0.237) and think length (+0.151) are strongest positive drivers; answer length is small (+0.038). Implement count (+0.088) is the strongest positive episode effect; Verify ratio is negative (−0.028).
Top positive transitions: Analyze→Implement (+0.092), Implement→Implement (+0.073), Implement→Analyze (+0.021), revealing an analysis-execution-refinement cycle.
Three representative SAT Math items show Easy (557 tokens, 25.3% Implement), Medium (795 tokens, 34.7% Implement), Hard (995 tokens, 40.3% Implement). Verify and Read shares decrease with difficulty.
Role-wise visualizations across solver profiles confirm consistent patterns: harder items produce longer traces and stronger concentration around Analyze and Implement.

claims.claim: Episode composition and transition dynamics provide complementary predictive process evidence beyond item text and reasoning length.

evidence.description: Combined Epi.+Tran. features show permutation importance 0.136 (exceeding Sem. at 0.084 and Len. at 0.042) and SHAP importance 1.024 (exceeding Len. at 0.526) under Both (Role) setting.
Full feature-importance results across all trace-source and aggregation settings show Epi.+Tran. consistently contributes nonzero importance.
Removing any single process feature family (Len./Epi./Tran.) consistently hurts performance. Sem. + FFea. (full) achieves best; Sem. Only lags by 7.4–12.1 F1 points.

claims.claim: Role-based aggregation (simulating weak/medium/strong students) produces more robust difficulty prediction than simply using more LRM rollouts.

evidence.description: Matched rollout-count control: Single Direct F1=0.708, Direct Replication (4x, same prompt) F1=0.714, Profiled Aggregation (4 profiles) F1=0.728. QwQ: Single Direct 0.681, Direct Replication 0.683, Profiled Aggregation 0.697.
Both (Role) achieves 0.730 ACC and 0.728 F1 vs. Both (without role) at 0.707/0.708. Role-based aggregation is more consistently beneficial than source-level aggregation.

claims.claim: A RoBERTa-based sentence classifier can reliably label LRM reasoning trace sentences into Schoenfeld's 8 cognitive episode types.

evidence.description: On human-annotated gold test set (7,067 sentences): accuracy 0.799, macro-F1 0.761, micro-F1 0.799.
Training corpus: 231,913 labeled sentence–episode pairs from GPT-5 annotations on 5 LRMs, cleaned and deduplicated.

claims.claim: Schoenfeld's Episode Theory provides a viable cognitive ontology for analyzing LRM reasoning traces in educational assessment.

evidence.description: Episode features (counts, ratios, and transitions across 8 episode types) consistently contribute predictive information across all experimental settings. Episode-distribution and transition features provide complementary signal to semantic and length features.
Episode-based interpretations align with educational psychology: harder items shift effort toward concrete solution execution (Implement) with iterative analysis (Analyze↔Implement transitions).

claims.claim: Process-based difficulty signals generalize across domains (math, verbal, reading, medical licensing) and task formats (classification, regression).

evidence.description: Epi2Diff achieves best results on SAT Math (math reasoning, classification), SAT Reading (verbal reasoning, classification), Cambridge (English reading, regression), and USMLE (medical, regression). This spans cognitive and domain diversity.

claims.claim: Simply scaling model capacity (larger models, more fine-tuning) does not reliably improve item difficulty prediction.

evidence.description: LLM zero-shot and ICL baselines often perform worse than simple SLM fine-tuning. Full-parameter SFT on 3B-4B models achieves 0.698 ACC at best on SAT Math, still below Epi2Diff's 0.730. GPT-4o/5 zero-shot and few-shot produce negative R² values on Cambridge and USMLE.
Prior work (Li et al., 2025a; Fulari & Rusert, 2024; Tack et al., 2024) also shows richer representations do not guarantee better predictions.
